You are ONE of N parallel CANDIDATE planners (candidate mode) for goal **G11** (code/test/doc-comment hygiene cleanup). Read `fetch_item("goals","G11")` and its 7 defects: D40 (CAP_NET_RAW pathsock comment vs CAP_NET_ADMIN systemd units mismatch — determine the real capability across supported kernels, align unit+comment+docs), D45 (`just lint` red at base: errcheck on unchecked Close in internal/dnsresolve/doh.go:206 + dot.go:168, staticcheck QF1001 in internal/bind/pathsock.go:166), D51 (e2e port collision: pacing_test.go + p3_fec_test.go both bind 127.0.0.1:9096), D54 (golangci-lint walks .claude/worktrees — set skip-dirs/exclude-dirs or lint an explicit package list), D56 (superseded PathSnapshots/FECSnapshot bind read seams + duplicated FEC-stat derivation — migrate bind tests to PeerSnapshots + delete, or thin wrappers), D57 (stale config.go Peer.PSK/Name 'not yet consumed' comments), D60 (stale config.go BindMode + Path.Bind 'config surface only' comments). Ground READ-ONLY against the cited files.

G11 is DEFECT-SEEDED, all low-severity mechanical. Produce a candidate task-DAG grouping into a SMALL number of coherent tasks minimizing worktree merge conflicts (co-locate same-file edits: D57+D60 both edit config.go → same task; D45+D54 both lint config/.golangci → group; D51 test port; D56 bind read seams; D40 systemd unit+pathsock comment+docs). Each fix per its defect suggestedFix; keep the gate green; each task ledgerRefs its defect(s) + goals:G11.

Return the candidate plan as a fenced ```json block ONLY (write NOTHING): { "milestones": [...], "tasks": [...], "rationale": "..." }. Prefer ~3-5 grouped tasks. End with a `### Session summary` block.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

18	// datapath as a SEND-side encoder and a RECEIVE-side decoder that composes with the
19	// T18 resequencer, T21 weighted scheduling, and T30 dynamic paths without regressing
20	// any of them. FEC is OFF unless a config carries an [fec] block (NewMultipath's

22	// behaviour.

24	// Wire mapping. The FEC data-shard bytes are OuterSeq || inner: the encoder codes
25	// parity over that, but the 8-byte outer-seq prefix is NOT transmitted — the DATA
26	// frame carries the outer-seq in its header and the inner datagram in its payload,
27	// and both sender and receiver reconstitute the shard bytes as
28	// header-seq || frame-payload. This keeps a recovered shard SELF-DESCRIBING (its
29	// reconstructed bytes carry its own outer-seq, so a frame reconstructed from parity
30	// resequences at its ORIGINAL outer-seq even when EVERY data frame of the group was
31	// lost) while adding only one wire byte per DATA frame (the shard index in the DATA
32	// header) and the group cardinality M per PARITY frame. See internal/frame.
33	//

35	// a single deadline-tick goroutine that TryLocks m.mu (never blocking, so it can

152	// from parity, and data shards lost beyond repair capacity). FEC being
153	// connection-scoped, these carry no path label. The frame overhead the operator cares
154	// about is ParityFrames/DataFrames, which tends to ParityShards/DataShards (M/K) once
155	// groups fill; both are counted only for frames that actually reached the socket, so
156	// the ratio reflects wire cost actually spent.
157	type FECStats struct {
158		DataFrames    uint64
159		DataBytes     uint64
160		ParityFrames  uint64
161		ParityBytes   uint64
162		Recovered     uint64
163		Unrecoverable uint64
164		// ResidualLoss is the current post-FEC-recovery connection loss fraction in [0,1]
165		// (T29): the share of outer-seqs neither natively received nor reconstructed from
166		// parity. It is the P4 acceptance signal — the loss FEC did not mask. Zero when FEC is
167		// disabled or no data has flowed.
168		ResidualLoss float64
169	}
170	
171	// fecShardPayload builds the FEC data-shard coded bytes for outer-seq seq over the
172	// opaque inner datagram: seq (8 BE) || inner. The sender codes parity over this; the
173	// receiver reconstitutes the identical bytes from the DATA frame header + payload so
174	// the decoder's shards match what the encoder coded.
175	func fecShardPayload(seq uint64, inner []byte) []byte {
176		b := make([]byte, fecSeqPrefixLen+len(inner))
177		binary.BigEndian.PutUint64(b, seq)
178		copy(b[fecSeqPrefixLen:], inner)
179		return b
180	}
181	
182	// splitFECShardPayload inverts fecShardPayload, returning the outer-seq and a fresh
183	// copy of the inner datagram. It fails fast on a shard too short to hold the prefix,
184	// which would indicate a decoder/encoder mismatch rather than a recoverable
185	// condition. The inner copy lets the resequencer take ownership without retaining the
186	// larger coded-shard backing array.
187	func splitFECShardPayload(b []byte) (uint64, []byte, error) {
188		if len(b) < fecSeqPrefixLen {
189			return 0, nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: recovered FEC shard %d bytes, shorter than the %d-byte outer-seq prefix", len(b), fecSeqPrefixLen)
190		}
191		seq := binary.BigEndian.Uint64(b[:fecSeqPrefixLen])
192		inner := make([]byte, len(b)-fecSeqPrefixLen)
193		copy(inner, b[fecSeqPrefixLen:])
194		return seq, inner, nil
195	}

103	//     route-based selection, voiding source_addr's pin — Criticism 2), and
104	//
105	//   - exactly ONE configured path resolves to that interface, because two
106	//     wildcard+device sockets on the same port and device collide EADDRINUSE;
107	//     source-IP binding keeps their two DISTINCT specific-IP sockets, which
108	//     coexist on one port — the pre-T16 behaviour (Criticism 1).

113	// srcs: a non-empty dev at i means "device-bind path i to it"; "" means
114	// "source-IP-bind path i". modes is parallel to srcs.
115	func selectDeviceBinds(srcs []netip.Addr, modes []config.BindMode, resolve func(netip.Addr) ifaceInfo) []string {
116		infos := make([]ifaceInfo, len(srcs))

119			infos[i] = resolve(s)
120			if infos[i].dev != "" {
121				devPaths[infos[i].dev]++
122			}
123		}
124		out := make([]string, len(srcs))
125		for i := range srcs {
126			info := infos[i]
127			switch modes[i] {
128			case config.BindModeSource:

130				// interface resolution would have decided (the D38 escape hatch).

133				// resolves. info.dev is already "" when unresolvable, which is the
134				// fallback-to-source-IP-binding outcome.
135				out[i] = info.dev
136			default: // config.BindModeAuto, or any other/unset value.
137				if info.dev != "" && info.familyCount == 1 && devPaths[info.dev] == 1 {
138					out[i] = info.dev

111	// requireLoopback enforces that addr's host is a loopback address. An IP literal
112	// must satisfy IsLoopback; a hostname (e.g. "localhost") is accepted only if it
113	// resolves to at least one address and EVERY resolved address is loopback — a
114	// hostname that maps to any routable address is refused. An empty host (":9095")
115	// binds every interface and is refused.
116	func requireLoopback(addr string) error {
117		host, _, err := net.SplitHostPort(addr)
118		if err != nil {
119			return fmt.Errorf("metrics: invalid listen address %q: %w", addr, err)
120		}
121		if host == "" {
122			return fmt.Errorf("%w: empty host in %q binds all interfaces", ErrNonLoopbackBind, addr)
123		}
124	
125		if ip, err := netip.ParseAddr(host); err == nil {
126			if !ip.IsLoopback() {
127				return fmt.Errorf("%w: got %q", ErrNonLoopbackBind, addr)
128			}
129			return nil
130		}
131	
132		// Hostname: resolve and require every resolved address to be loopback.
133		ips, err := net.LookupIP(host)
134		if err != nil {
135			return fmt.Errorf("metrics: cannot resolve listen host %q: %w", host, err)
136		}
137		if len(ips) == 0 {
138			return fmt.Errorf("metrics: listen host %q resolved to no addresses", host)
139		}
140		for _, ip := range ips {
141			if !ip.IsLoopback() {
142				return fmt.Errorf("%w: host %q resolves to non-loopback %s", ErrNonLoopbackBind, host, ip)
143			}
144		}
145		return nil
146	}

148	// verifyLoopbackBind is the fail-closed, act-then-verify half of the loopback
149	// guard: it asserts that the address the kernel ACTUALLY bound (ln.Addr()) is a
150	// loopback TCP address, independent of any DNS resolution. Because net.Listen
151	// resolves a hostname listen address itself — a second, independent lookup from
152	// requireLoopback's — verifying the concrete bound address is the only check
153	// that a resolver cannot subvert. A non-TCP or non-loopback bound address
154	// yields ErrNonLoopbackBind so the caller can close the listener and refuse.
155	func verifyLoopbackBind(a net.Addr) error {
156		tcp, ok := a.(*net.TCPAddr)
157		if !ok {
158			return fmt.Errorf("%w: bound to non-TCP address %v", ErrNonLoopbackBind, a)
159		}
160		if !tcp.IP.IsLoopback() {
161			return fmt.Errorf("%w: bound to non-loopback %s", ErrNonLoopbackBind, tcp.IP)
162		}
163		return nil
164	}

# wanbond task runner. Targets assume the dev shell is active (`nix develop`),
# which puts go, golangci-lint, and the netem/DPI tooling on PATH.

# Show available targets.
default:
    @just --list

# Build all non-privileged packages.
build:
    go build ./...

# Cross-compile static release binaries (CGO_ENABLED=0, stripped, version
# stamped from git) for linux/amd64 and linux/arm64 into dist/. `file` reports
# them statically linked; see docs/install.md for the deployment steps.
release:
    mkdir -p dist
    CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux GOARCH=amd64 go build -trimpath -ldflags "-s -w -X main.version=$(git describe --tags --always --dirty)" -o dist/wanbond-linux-amd64 ./cmd/wanbond
    CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux GOARCH=arm64 go build -trimpath -ldflags "-s -w -X main.version=$(git describe --tags --always --dirty)" -o dist/wanbond-linux-arm64 ./cmd/wanbond

# The e2e/realhosts sources are gated behind //go:build tags, so a tagless
# vet/lint never compiles them and silently skips them (see D28). The tagged
# passes below are additive — the tagless run still analyses the default build
# exactly as before.
# Vet + lint the default build plus the e2e- and realhosts-tagged sources.
lint:
    go vet ./...
    go vet -tags e2e ./test/e2e/...
    go vet -tags realhosts ./test/realhosts/...
    golangci-lint run
    golangci-lint run --build-tags e2e ./test/e2e/...
    golangci-lint run --build-tags realhosts ./test/realhosts/...

# Format check (fails if anything is unformatted).
fmt-check:
    test -z "$(gofmt -l cmd internal test)"

# Unprivileged unit/property tests only (the e2e build tag is never set here, so
# no privileged test compiles or runs).
test:
    go test ./...

# Privileged end-to-end suite: netns/netem + TUN tunnel bring-up. Requires root
# (CAP_NET_ADMIN + /dev/net/tun); run on real or emulated hardware, not CI.
e2e:
    sudo -E go test -tags e2e ./test/e2e/... -count=1

# Run one named e2e phase test, e.g. `just e2e-run TestP1Failover`.
e2e-run TEST:
    sudo -E go test -tags e2e ./test/e2e/... -run {{TEST}} -count=1 -v

# Opt-in real-host e2e tier: drive the two standing worker machines (edge behind
# symmetric NAT, aarch64 concentrator) over SSH. Report-only — it records host
# uname/arch and gates nothing. Needs the `llm` SSH key (WANBOND_SSH_KEY, default
# /run/agenix/llm-ssh-key); no root required. NEVER part of `just test` or CI.
# Run all: `just realhosts`; one test: `just realhosts TestRealConnectivity`.
realhosts TEST="":
    go test -tags realhosts ./test/realhosts/... {{ if TEST != "" { "-run " + TEST } else { "" } }} -count=1 -v

# Opt-in idempotent real-host provisioning: ensure iperf3 + gcc + Go 1.26.x on
# both hosts and the concentrator's tunnel-interface INPUT ACCEPT rule. Report-
# only, idempotent (a second run reports no changes); same SSH key requirements
# as `realhosts`. Thin wrapper over TestRealProvision. NEVER part of `just test`.
realhosts-provision:
    go test -tags realhosts ./test/realhosts/... -run TestRealProvision -count=1 -v

# P0 real-link baseline: the SINGLE repeatable pre-pilot procedure (docs/manual-
# checklist.md §P0). A thin orchestration layer over three EXISTING realhosts
# tests — it provisions both ends, brings the tunnel up, and records:
#   - TestRealP0Smoke              single-uplink handshake + ping RTT + iperf3
#                                  (single/8x-parallel TCP, UDP goodput/loss);
#   - TestRealAggregationBufferbloat  per-path + bonded throughput and their
#                                  aggregation ratio, plus idle-vs-loaded RTT
#                                  bufferbloat delta under saturating load;
#   - TestRealMidTransferWANKill   mid-transfer LINK failover and HUB failover
#                                  (T57) recovery timings.
# It TEES the full -v output to a timestamped baseline report under
# test/realhosts/reports/ (gitignored). REPORT-ONLY (Q19): the underlying tests
# assert LIVENESS ONLY — no Mbit/s or ms threshold gates the run; the numbers are
# informational for the operator's (non-blocking) pilot-gate decision. Same SSH
# key requirement as `realhosts` (WANBOND_SSH_KEY, default /run/agenix/llm-ssh-
# key). NEVER part of `just test` or CI. A non-zero exit means the run itself
# could not complete (host unreachable, tunnel never came up), NOT that a
# performance number missed a target.
p0-baseline:
    #!/usr/bin/env bash
    set -euo pipefail
    mkdir -p test/realhosts/reports
    report="test/realhosts/reports/p0-baseline-$(date -u +%Y%m%dT%H%M%SZ).log"
    echo "wanbond P0 real-link baseline — $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)" | tee "$report"
    go test -tags realhosts ./test/realhosts/... \
        -run '^(TestRealP0Smoke|TestRealAggregationBufferbloat|TestRealMidTransferWANKill)$' \
        -count=1 -v 2>&1 | tee -a "$report"
    echo "baseline report: $report" | tee -a "$report"

232	// should also egress on, per the installed FECPolicy over the current eligible set.
233	// It returns nil when no policy is installed (no FEC). It is the P3 hook a redundant-
234	// transmission Bind would call alongside Pick; today it is a documented, unit-tested
235	// seam with no in-tree caller on the hot path.
236	func (s *WeightedScheduler) RedundantPaths(chosen int) []int {
237		s.mu.Lock()
238		defer s.mu.Unlock()
239		if s.fec == nil {
240			return nil
241		}
242		return s.fec.RedundantPaths(chosen, s.eligibleLocked())
243	}
244	
245	// Pick selects the path for the NEXT frame and advances all per-frame distribution
246	// state (offered-load meter, aggregation gate, pacing buckets, round-robin credits).
247	// It returns a negative value when no path is eligible OR when every eligible path
248	// is paced out (the frame is dropped rather than queued — pacing bounds egress and,
249	// by dropping the overflow, bounds the send backlog). It is safe for concurrent
250	// callers.
251	func (s *WeightedScheduler) Pick(class FrameClass) int {
252		now := s.clock.Now()
253		s.mu.Lock()
254		defer s.mu.Unlock()
255	
256		// Capture the inter-sample idle gap BEFORE observeLoadLocked advances lastLoad: a
257		// long gap since the previous offered frame is itself the strongest evidence of low
258		// load, and the collapse hysteresis must count it (criticism #1 — otherwise an
259		// abrupt overload stop leaves the gate engaged across the whole idle span and the
260		// next low burst dribbles onto the metered path).
261		gap := s.loadGapLocked(now)
262		s.recomputeLocked(now)       // liveness refresh + eager-failover log
263		s.observeLoadLocked(now)     // this Pick is one offered frame (decays across the gap)
264		s.updateGateLocked(now, gap) // engage/disengage aggregation (hysteresis, idle-aware)
265		s.refillLocked(now)          // top up pacing buckets
266	
267		eligible := s.eligibleLocked()
268		if len(eligible) == 0 {
269			return PickNone
270		}
271		if !s.aggregating {
272			// Data-thrift: at low load only the primary (best eligible) carries traffic;
273			// the metered backup stays idle. A path-down excludes it from eligible, so
274			// eligible[0] is the surviving highest-priority path — failover is preserved.
275			return s.serveLocked(now, eligible[0], class)
276		}
277		weights := s.weightsLocked(eligible)
278		return s.selectAggregatingLocked(now, eligible, weights, class)
279	}
280	
281	// loadGapLocked returns the wall-clock gap since the previous offered-load sample, or
282	// 0 before the first sample. Caller holds s.mu.
283	func (s *WeightedScheduler) loadGapLocked(now time.Time) time.Duration {
284		if !s.haveLoad {
285			return 0
286		}
287		gap := now.Sub(s.lastLoad)
288		if gap < 0 {
289			gap = 0
290		}
291		return gap
292	}
293	
294	// Recompute refreshes only the liveness-derived eligible/active set from the current
295	// PathHealth and logs any active-path transition. It advances NONE of the per-frame
296	// distribution state (load meter, gate, pacing, round-robin credits), so the eager-
297	// failover nudge (defect D18/T40) drives an egress-lull failover recompute through it
298	// WITHOUT stealing a weighted-distribution slot the way a spurious Pick would. It is
299	// safe for concurrent callers.
300	func (s *WeightedScheduler) Recompute() {
301		now := s.clock.Now()
302		s.mu.Lock()
303		defer s.mu.Unlock()
304		s.recomputeLocked(now)
305	}
306	
307	// recomputeLocked refreshes the active-primary cache (best eligible path) and logs a
308	// transition on change. Caller holds s.mu.
309	func (s *WeightedScheduler) recomputeLocked(_ time.Time) {
310		best := s.bestEligibleLocked()
311		s.setActiveLocked(best)
312	}
313	
314	// serveLocked applies pacing to a single chosen path: it returns idx when the frame
315	// is admitted, or PickPaced when pacing is on and the path has no token (the frame is
316	// shed — dropped, bounding egress and backlog — and the shedding diagnostic is
317	// rate-limited). A ClassControl frame is pacing-EXEMPT (defect D22): it egresses on
318	// idx unconditionally, spending no token and never shed, so bulk overload cannot
319	// starve WireGuard rekey. Caller holds s.mu.
320	func (s *WeightedScheduler) serveLocked(now time.Time, idx int, class FrameClass) int {
321		if class == ClassControl {
322			return idx
323		}
324		if s.tryConsumeLocked(idx) {
325			return idx
326		}
327		return s.shedLocked(now)
328	}
329	
330	// selectAggregatingLocked picks a path proportional to weight (smooth weighted round
331	// robin) and applies pacing. When the round-robin's ideal pick is paced out it falls
```

# wanbond — install and operations

wanbond ships as a single static binary. One binary serves both roles — the
**edge** (the mobile Linux box bonding the WAN uplinks) and the **concentrator**
(the public-IP VPS terminating the tunnel); the role is selected by the `role`
key in the config file, never by which binary is invoked.

> **Provisioning a fresh deployment end-to-end?** Follow the
> **[pre-pilot rollout runbook](runbook.md)** — a top-to-bottom operator
> procedure (key/PSK generation, both-ends config, standby-concentrator hub
> failover, firewall persistence, pacing, and health checks) that ties the
> sections below together. This document is the per-topic reference the runbook
> points back into.

## 1. Build the release binaries

From the repo root, inside the dev shell:

```sh
nix develop -c just release
```

This cross-compiles `cmd/wanbond` with `CGO_ENABLED=0` (fully static, no libc
dependency) for both deployment architectures into `dist/`:

- `dist/wanbond-linux-amd64` — x86_64 (typical OCI/AWS VPS concentrator)
- `dist/wanbond-linux-arm64` — aarch64 (ARM edge boxes, Ampere VPSes)

Verify with `file dist/*`: both must report `statically linked`. Alternatively
`nix build` produces the host-architecture binary via the flake package.

## 2. Install the binary

On each host (pick the artifact matching `uname -m`):

```sh
install -m 0755 wanbond-linux-<arch> /usr/local/bin/wanbond
wanbond version   # prints the stamped build version
```

## 3. Write the config file — 0600 REQUIRED

The daemon takes exactly one flag: `--config <path>`. The systemd units use
`/etc/wanbond/edge.toml` and `/etc/wanbond/concentrator.toml`.

The file holds the WireGuard private key and the outer-control PSK, so
`config.Load` **refuses any file whose permission bits are not exactly `0600`**
(`insecure permissions` error at startup). Create it as:

```sh
mkdir -p /etc/wanbond
touch /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
chown root:root /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
chmod 0600 /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
```

### Edge config (`/etc/wanbond/edge.toml`)

```toml
role = "edge"
psk = "<base64 32-byte outer-control PSK, same on both ends>"

# One [[paths]] block per WAN uplink. source_addr pins the path's UDP socket
# to the local source IP the upstream router routes out the intended WAN.
[[paths]]
name = "starlink"
source_addr = "192.168.1.10"

[[paths]]
name = "5g"
source_addr = "192.168.2.10"
# dest_addr = "203.0.113.7:51820"  # optional per-path concentrator endpoint;
                                   # omit when one public IP fronts all uplinks

[wireguard]
private_key = "<base64 edge private key>"

[[wireguard.peers]]
public_key = "<base64 concentrator public key>"
endpoint = "203.0.113.7:51820"     # required on the edge; an ip:port literal
                                   # (default) OR a hostname:port behind the
                                   # opt-in `dns = true` below — see §Optional
                                   # [dns] resolver block.
# dns = true                        # OPTIONAL, default false. Enables hostname
# resolution for the endpoint above (e.g. endpoint = "hub.example.com:51820").
# The [dns] block is itself OPTIONAL: absent, hostnames resolve through the
# OS system resolver; see §Optional [dns] resolver block below.
# endpoints = ["203.0.113.7:51820", "198.51.100.7:51820"]  # ordered hub-failover
# form (Q18): first entry is the active/primary concentrator, the rest are
# ordered standbys tried in order when the active one is lost. Mutually
# exclusive with `endpoint` above — `endpoint` is just its one-element form,
# so a single-concentrator deployment keeps using it unchanged. Edge-only.

239	  operator must hand-tune it per (loss, K) to clear a given SLA (the reason
240	  `target_residual` supersedes it). Defaults to 1.5 when adaptive and neither
241	  field is set; note 1.5 sizes M=1 at 5% loss with K=10 (~1% residual), so a

288	block:

297	```

300	  it never turns hostname resolution on by itself.

```sh

only one path at a time, or isolate it at the router layer. The measurement must

Also measure the RTT under load (as a sanity check on bufferbloat):

```sh
# While iperf3 is running (in another terminal on the edge):
ping -i 0.2 10.77.0.1
```

```toml

[[paths]]
name = "5g"
source_addr = "192.168.2.10"

link_rtt = "45ms"            # higher baseline latency
```

- **Declare on every path or none.** If you declare `link_bandwidth` on one path,

- **Round conservatively.** Round down if unsure (e.g., measure 49.8 Mbit/s →

```sh
journalctl -u wanbond-edge -n 20

A successful load will log `config loaded` (or, on reload, `config reloaded`);

```sh

561	default instead of repeating `bind = "source"` on each `[[paths]]` block — a path

575	
576	Accepted `bind` values (top-level default, and per-path override) are:
577	
578	- `"source"` — force source-IP binding (defeats device-bind roam tolerance, safe for
579	  policy-routed topologies)
580	- `"device"` — force device binding (requires manual rule workaround for policy routing)
581	- `"auto"` (default) — automatic heuristic: device-bind only when the source resolves to
582	  a non-loopback interface, that interface carries exactly one address of the source's
583	  family, AND exactly one configured path resolves to that interface (two paths sharing
584	  one device fall back to source binding, since two wildcard+device sockets on the same
585	  port would collide `EADDRINUSE`); every other case source-binds.

594	
595	This is the exhaustive key list — every configuration key wanbond reads, in one
596	place. The example below is **edge-oriented**; concentrator-only keys are shown
597	and marked `CONCENTRATOR-ONLY`. Required keys are shown live; optional/defaulted
598	keys are shown **commented-out with their default value**. Uncommenting a key
599	and leaving it at the shown value is a no-op. The per-section notes after the
600	example capture the cross-field constraints a single example cannot express
601	inline. All of these are enforced at config load (`config.Load`), which fails
602	fast on the first violation — except the loopback and `allowed_ips` checks,

If every path in a VLAN-per-WAN topology needs source binding, set the top-level
default instead of repeating `bind = "source"` on each `[[paths]]` block — a path
only needs its own `bind` when it deviates from the default:

```toml
bind = "source"

[[paths]]
name = "starlink"
source_addr = "192.168.1.10"

[[paths]]
name = "5g"
source_addr = "192.168.2.10"
```

Accepted `bind` values (top-level default, and per-path override) are:

- `"source"` — force source-IP binding (defeats device-bind roam tolerance, safe for
  policy-routed topologies)
- `"device"` — force device binding (requires manual rule workaround for policy routing)
- `"auto"` (default) — automatic heuristic: device-bind only when the source resolves to
  a non-loopback interface, that interface carries exactly one address of the source's
  family, AND exactly one configured path resolves to that interface (two paths sharing
  one device fall back to source binding, since two wildcard+device sockets on the same
  port would collide `EADDRINUSE`); every other case source-binds.

Restart the daemon after changing the config:

```sh
systemctl restart wanbond-edge
```

956	#### Persisting `wanbond0` across daemon restarts (`tun_persist`, I7)
957

965	issues `TUNSETPERSIST` on start, so the kernel keeps `wanbond0` across a daemon

967	device by name rather than recreating it. Addresses, routes, and rules

969	`tun_persist = false` and restarting clears the flag, so the device returns to

Allow the WireGuard UDP port in from the WAN (and, on OCI, in the subnet's
security list / NSG as well — both layers must pass):

```sh
iptables -I INPUT -p udp --dport 51820 -j ACCEPT
```

### Concentrator: tunnel-interface ACCEPT — REQUIRED (OCI default-REJECT caveat)

# wanbond edge role: the mobile Linux box that bonds the WAN uplinks.
# Install: cp wanbond-edge.service /etc/systemd/system/ && systemctl daemon-reload
# Config:  /etc/wanbond/edge.toml, root-owned, mode 0600 EXACTLY (the daemon
#          refuses anything else — the file holds the WG private key and PSK).
# See docs/install.md for addressing/routing drop-ins and firewall requirements.

[Unit]
Description=wanbond bonded WAN tunnel (edge role)
After=network-online.target
Wants=network-online.target
ConditionPathExists=/etc/wanbond/edge.toml

[Service]
Type=exec
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/wanbond --config /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
# SIGHUP re-reads the config and applies the path diff (add/remove uplinks)
# without tearing down the live tunnel.
ExecReload=/bin/kill -HUP $MAINPID
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=2s

# The daemon needs only CAP_NET_ADMIN (TUN device creation); it does no
# interface addressing or routing itself (see docs/install.md).
CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN
DeviceAllow=/dev/net/tun rw
NoNewPrivileges=yes
ProtectSystem=strict
ProtectHome=yes
PrivateTmp=yes
ProtectKernelTunables=yes
ProtectKernelModules=yes
ProtectKernelLogs=yes
ProtectControlGroups=yes
ProtectClock=yes
ProtectHostname=yes
RestrictAddressFamilies=AF_INET AF_INET6 AF_NETLINK AF_UNIX
RestrictNamespaces=yes
RestrictRealtime=yes
RestrictSUIDSGID=yes
LockPersonality=yes
MemoryDenyWriteExecute=yes
SystemCallArchitectures=native
SystemCallFilter=@system-service
UMask=0077

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

The systemd units live at packaging/systemd/ (edge + concentrator + addressing@), run as root with CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN.
- **Issues:** none — wrote nothing to any ledger; candidate returned below for the synthesis judge.
